cards.front: State the definition of Vector space.
cards.back:
A set $V$ is a \emph{vector space} over $\mathbb{R}$ if it is closed under addition and scalar multiplication, i.e.
\[
 u,v\in V \implies u+v\in V, \qquad \lambda\in\mathbb{R},\ v\in V \implies \lambda v\in V,
\]
and contains a zero element.

cards.front: State the definition of Subspace.
cards.back:
A subset $U\subseteq V$ is a \emph{subspace} if it is itself a vector space under the induced operations; equivalently, $U\neq\varnothing$ and for all $u,v\in U$ and $\lambda\in\mathbb{R}$,
\[
 u+v\in U, \qquad \lambda u\in U.
\]

cards.front: State the definition of Norm.
State the definition of Normed vector space.
cards.back: Given a vector space $V$ and a norm $\|\cdot\|$, the pair $(V,\|\cdot\|)$ is called a \emph{normed vector space}.

cards.front: State the definition of Equivalent norms.
cards.back:
Two norms $\|\cdot\|_a$ and $\|\cdot\|_b$ on $V$ are \emph{equivalent} if there exist constants $0<c\le C<\infty$ such that for all $v\in V$,
\[
 c\|v\|_a \le \|v\|_b \le C\|v\|_a.
\]

cards.front: State the theorem of Finite-dimensional norm equivalence.
cards.back: On any finite-dimensional vector space, all norms are equivalent.

cards.front: State the definition of Inner product.
State the theorem of Inner products induce norms.
cards.back:
If $\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle$ is an inner product on $V$, then
\[
 \|v\| := \sqrt{\langle v,v\rangle}
\]
defines a norm on $V$.

cards.front: State the theorem of Completeness of $C[a,b.
cards.back:
$ with the sup norm]
The space
\[
 C[a,b] := \{f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}: f \text{ continuous}\}
\]
is complete when equipped with the supremum norm
\[
 \|f\|_\infty := \sup_{x\in[a,b]} |f(x)|.
\]

cards.front: State the definition of Open ball.
cards.back:
For a normed space $(V,\|\cdot\|)$, the open ball centered at $u\in V$ of radius $r>0$ is
\[
 B_r(u):=\{v\in V:\|v-u\|<r\}.
\]

cards.front: State the definition of Convergent sequence.
cards.back:
A sequence $v^{(n)}$ in a normed space converges to $v$ if
\[
 \|v^{(n)}-v\|\to 0 \quad \text{as } n\to\infty.
\]

cards.front: State the definition of Closed set and closure.
cards.back: A set $S\subseteq V$ is \emph{closed} if it contains all limits of convergent sequences in $S$. The \emph{closure} of $S$, denoted $\overline S$, is the set obtained by adjoining to $S$ all limits of convergent sequences from $S$.

cards.front: State the definition of Cauchy sequence.
cards.back:
A sequence $v^{(n)}$ in a normed space is \emph{Cauchy} if for every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $N\in\mathbb{N}$ such that for all $m,n\ge N$,
\[
 \|v^{(n)}-v^{(m)}\|<\varepsilon.
\]

cards.front: State the definition of Complete space, Banach space, Hilbert space.
cards.back: A normed vector space is \emph{complete} if every Cauchy sequence converges in the space. A complete normed vector space is a \emph{Banach space}. A complete inner product space is a \emph{Hilbert space}.

cards.front: State the definition of Convex function.
cards.back:
Let $X$ be a vector space and $C\subseteq X$ be convex. A function $f:C\to\mathbb{R}$ is \emph{convex} if for all $u,v\in C$ and all $\theta\in[0,1]$,
\[
 f(\theta u+(1-\theta)v) \le \theta f(u)+(1-\theta)f(v).
\]

cards.front: State the definition of Conjugate exponents.
cards.back:
Numbers $p,q\in[1,\infty]$ are \emph{conjugate} if
\[
 \frac1p+\frac1q=1.
\]

cards.front: State the theorem of Finite-dimensional completeness.
cards.back: Every finite-dimensional normed vector space is complete.

cards.front: State the theorem of $\ell^p$ is Banach.
cards.back: For every $p\in[1,\infty]$, the sequence space $\ell^p$ is a Banach space.

cards.front: State the definition of Linear operator.
cards.back:
Let $(V,\|\cdot\|_V)$ and $(W,\|\cdot\|_W)$ be normed spaces. A map $L:V\to W$ is \emph{linear} if for all $u,v\in V$ and $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$,
\[
 L(\alpha u+v)=\alpha L(u)+L(v).
\]

cards.front: State the definition of Bounded linear operator.
cards.back:
A linear map $L:V\to W$ is \emph{bounded} if there exists $K\ge 0$ such that
\[
 \|Lv\|_W\le K\|v\|_V \qquad \forall v\in V.
\]

cards.front: State the definition of Operator norm.
cards.back:
For a bounded linear operator $L:V\to W$, its operator norm is
\[
 \|L\|_{\mathcal L(V,W)}:=\sup_{v\ne 0}\frac{\|Lv\|_W}{\|v\|_V}=\sup_{\|v\|_V=1}\|Lv\|_W.
\]

cards.front: State the theorem of Bounded operators form a Banach space.
cards.back: If $W$ is Banach, then $\mathcal L(V,W)$ equipped with the operator norm is a Banach space.

cards.front: State the definition of Outer product.
cards.back:
Let $H$ be a Hilbert space and $a,b\in H$. The \emph{outer product} $a\otimes b\in\mathcal L(H,H)$ is defined by
\[
 (a\otimes b)(x)=\langle b,x\rangle a.
\]

cards.front: State the definition of Dual space.
cards.back:
If $(X,\|\cdot\|)$ is a normed vector space, its \emph{dual space} is
\[
 X^*:=\mathcal L(X,\mathbb{R}),
\]
with norm
\[
 \|f\|_{X^*}:=\sup_{x\ne 0}\frac{|f(x)|}{\|x\|}.
\]

cards.front: State the definition of Isometric isomorphism.
cards.back:
Normed spaces $(X,\|\cdot\|_X)$ and $(Y,\|\cdot\|_Y)$ are \emph{isometrically isomorphic} if there exists a bijective linear map $L:X\to Y$ such that
\[
 \|Lx\|_Y=\|x\|_X \qquad \forall x\in X.
\]

cards.front: State the theorem of Dual of $\ell^q$.
cards.back:
If $1\le q<\infty$ and $p$ is the conjugate exponent to $q$, then
\[
 (\ell^q)^* \cong \ell^p
\]
isometrically. More precisely, every $w\in\ell^p$ defines a bounded linear functional on $\ell^q$ by
\[
 f_w(v)=\sum_{j=1}^\infty v_j w_j,
\]
and every element of $(\ell^q)^*$ is of this form.

cards.front: State the definition of Standard basis in $\ell^p$.
cards.back: For $1\le p<\infty$, let $e^{(n)}\in\ell^p$ denote the sequence with $(e^{(n)})_j=\delta_{jn}$. Then $\|e^{(n)}\|_{\ell^p}=1$.

cards.front: State the theorem of Hahn--Banach.
cards.back:
Let $(X,\|\cdot\|)$ be a normed space, let $U\subseteq X$ be a subspace, and let $f:U\to\mathbb{R}$ be linear and bounded. Then there exists an extension $F:X\to\mathbb{R}$ such that
\[
 F|_U=f, \qquad \|F\|_{X^*}=\|f\|_{U^*}.
\]

cards.front: State the theorem of Separation of points by dual functionals.
cards.back:
If $x,y\in X$ and $x\ne y$, then there exists $f\in X^*$ such that
\[
 f(x)\ne f(y).
\]
Equivalently, for every nonzero $x\in X$ there exists $f\in X^*$ with $f(x)\ne 0$.

cards.front: State the definition of Weak convergence.
cards.back:
A sequence $x^{(n)}$ in a Banach space $X$ converges \emph{weakly} to $x\in X$, written $x^{(n)}\rightharpoonup x$, if for every $f\in X^*$,
\[
 f(x^{(n)})\to f(x).
\]

cards.front: State the theorem of Weak convergence implies boundedness.
cards.back: If $x^{(n)}\rightharpoonup x$ in a Banach space $X$, then the sequence $\{x^{(n)}\}$ is bounded in norm.

cards.front: State the definition of Dense subset, separable space, closure.
State the theorem of Characterization of separability.
cards.back: A normed space $X$ is separable if and only if it contains a countable dense subset.

cards.front: State the theorem of $\ell^p$-inclusion.
cards.back:
If $1\le p\le s\le\infty$, then
\[
 \ell^p \subseteq \ell^s,
\]
and for every $u\in\ell^p$,
\[
 \|u\|_{\ell^s}\le \|u\|_{\ell^p}.
\]

cards.front: State the definition of Continuous embedding.
cards.back:
Let $(X,\|\cdot\|_X)$ and $(Y,\|\cdot\|_Y)$ be normed spaces with $X\subseteq Y$. We say that $X$ is \emph{continuously embedded} in $Y$, and write
\[
 X\hookrightarrow Y,
\]
if there exists $C>0$ such that
\[
 \|x\|_Y\le C\|x\|_X \qquad \forall x\in X.
\]

cards.front: State the theorem of Norm inequality criterion for continuous embedding.
cards.back:
If $X\subseteq Y$, then the inclusion map $i:X\to Y$, $i(x)=x$, is bounded if and only if there exists $C>0$ such that
\[
 \|x\|_Y\le C\|x\|_X \qquad \forall x\in X.
\]

cards.front: State the definition of Equivalent normed spaces.
cards.back: If $X$ and $Y$ are vector spaces with the same underlying set and each is continuously embedded in the other, we say that they are \emph{equivalent} as normed spaces.

cards.front: State the theorem of Equivalent spaces and equivalent norms.
cards.back: If $X$ and $Y$ have the same underlying vector space, then $X$ and $Y$ are equivalent iff their norms are equivalent.

cards.front: State the definition of Weighted sequence spaces $X^s$.
cards.back:
For $s\in\mathbb{R}$, define
\[
 X^s:=\Bigl\{v=(v_j)_{j\ge1}: \sum_{j=1}^\infty j^{2s}|v_j|^2<\infty\Bigr\},
\]
with norm
\[
 \|v\|_{X^s}^2:=\sum_{j=1}^\infty j^{2s}|v_j|^2.
\]

cards.front: State the theorem of Continuous embedding of weighted spaces.
cards.back: For $s>0$, $X^s$ is continuously embedded into $\ell^r$ for suitable $r$, in particular into $\ell^2$, and the embedding gains summability as $s$ increases. (In the handwritten notes this is stated in the form $X^s\subset \ell^r$ for $r$ in the admissible range.)

cards.front: State the definition of Compact set in a Banach space.
cards.back: A subset $B\subset X$ of a Banach space is \emph{compact} if every sequence in $B$ has a convergent subsequence with limit in $B$.

cards.front: State the definition of Relatively compact subset.
cards.back: A subset $S\subset X$ is \emph{relatively compact} if its closure is compact.

cards.front: State the definition of Compact operator.
cards.back: A linear map $L:X\to Y$ between Banach spaces is \emph{compact} if it maps bounded subsets of $X$ to relatively compact subsets of $Y$.

cards.front: State the theorem of Characterization of compact operators.
cards.back: A linear operator $L:X\to Y$ is compact if and only if for every bounded sequence $\{x_n\}\subset X$, the sequence $\{Lx_n\}\subset Y$ has a convergent subsequence.

cards.front: State the theorem of Compact operators are bounded.
cards.back: If $L:X\to Y$ is compact and linear, then $L\in\mathcal L(X,Y)$.

cards.front: State the theorem of Composition with bounded maps preserves compactness.
cards.back: Let $X,Y,Z$ be Banach spaces. If $A\in\mathcal L(Y,Z)$ and $L\in\mathcal C(X,Y)$ is compact, then $A\circ L\in\mathcal C(X,Z)$. Likewise, if $L\in\mathcal C(X,Y)$ and $B\in\mathcal L(W,X)$, then $L\circ B\in\mathcal C(W,Y)$.

cards.front: State the definition of Compact embedding.
cards.back:
We say that $X$ is \emph{compactly embedded} in $Y$, written
\[
 X\Subset Y,
\]
if the inclusion map $X\to Y$ is compact.

cards.front: State the theorem of Transitivity of compact embedding.
cards.back: If $X\Subset Y$ and $Y\Subset Z$, then $X\Subset Z$.

cards.front: State the theorem of Compact plus continuous gives compact.
cards.back: If $X\Subset Y$ and $Y\hookrightarrow Z$, then $X\Subset Z$.

cards.front: State the theorem of Compactness turns weak convergence into strong convergence.
cards.back:
If $X\Subset Y$ and $x_n\rightharpoonup x$ in $X$, then
\[
 x_n\to x \quad \text{strongly in } Y.
\]

cards.front: State the definition of Schauder basis.
cards.back:
A sequence $(\varphi_j)_{j\ge1}$ in a Hilbert space $H$ is a \emph{Schauder basis} if every $v\in H$ admits a unique expansion
\[
 v=\sum_{j=1}^\infty v_j\varphi_j.
\]

cards.front: State the theorem of Closest point theorem.
cards.back:
Let $U\subset H$ be a nonempty closed convex set in a Hilbert space $H$. Then for every $z\in H$ there exists a unique $u\in U$ such that
\[
 \|z-u\| = \inf_{v\in U} \|z-v\|.
\]

cards.front: State the definition of Orthogonality and orthogonal complement.
cards.back:
For $u,v\in H$, we say that $u$ is \emph{orthogonal} to $v$, written $u\perp v$, if $\langle u,v\rangle=0$. For a subset $U\subset H$, its \emph{orthogonal complement} is
\[
 U^\perp := \{v\in H: \langle v,u\rangle=0\ \forall u\in U\}.
\]

cards.front: State the definition of Complete orthonormal basis.
cards.back: A family $(\varphi^{(j)})_{j\ge1}$ is a \emph{complete orthonormal basis} (ONB) of $H$ if it is orthonormal and its linear span is dense in $H$.

cards.front: State the theorem of Orthogonal decomposition.
cards.back:
If $U\subset H$ is a closed subspace, then every $v\in H$ has a unique decomposition
\[
 v=u+w, \qquad u\in U,\ w\in U^\perp,
\]
so that
\[
 H=U\oplus U^\perp.
\]
Moreover, $u$ is the closest point in $U$ to $v$.

cards.front: State the definition of Orthogonal projection.
cards.back:
Given a closed subspace $U\subset H$, the \emph{orthogonal projection} onto $U$ is the map $P_U:H\to H$ defined by
\[
 P_U v = u,
\]
where $v=u+w$ with $u\in U$ and $w\in U^\perp$.

cards.front: State the theorem of Properties of orthogonal projections.
cards.back:
For a closed subspace $U\subset H$, the orthogonal projection $P_U$ is linear, bounded, satisfies $\|P_U\|\le 1$, and obeys
\[
 P_U^2=P_U, \qquad P_U^*=P_U.
\]

cards.front: State the theorem of Characterizations of ONB.
State the definition of Adjoint.
cards.back:
Let $H_1,H_2$ be Hilbert spaces and $T\in\mathcal L(H_1,H_2)$. The \emph{adjoint} $T^*\in\mathcal L(H_2,H_1)$ is defined by
\[
 \langle Tu,v\rangle_{H_2}=\langle u,T^*v\rangle_{H_1}
\qquad \forall u\in H_1,\ v\in H_2.
\]

cards.front: State the definition of Numerical range.
cards.back:
For $A\in\mathcal L(H)$, the \emph{numerical range} is
\[
 \Sigma(A):=\{\langle Au,u\rangle: \|u\|=1\}.
\]
For symmetric operators the handwritten notes define a lower bound parameter by
\[
 \lambda(A):=\inf \Sigma(A).
\]

cards.front: State the theorem of Norm from the numerical range for positive symmetric operators.
cards.back:
If $A\in\mathcal L(H)$ is positive and symmetric, then
\[
 \|A\|_{\mathcal L(H)} = \sup \Sigma(A).
\]

cards.front: State the definition of Positive operator.
cards.back:
A symmetric operator $A\in\mathcal L(H)$ is \emph{positive} if
\[
 \langle Au,u\rangle \ge 0 \qquad \forall u\in H.
\]
Equivalently, its spectrum lies in $[0,\infty)$.

cards.front: State the theorem of Spectral information for symmetric compact operators.
cards.back: If $A\in\mathcal L(H)$ is symmetric and compact, then the nonzero eigenvalues of $A$ are real, and the corresponding eigenspaces yield an orthonormal basis of the closure of the range generated by eigenvectors.

cards.front: State the definition of Hilbert--Schmidt norm.
cards.back:
Let $(\varphi^{(j)})_{j\ge1}$ be an ONB of $H$. For $T\in\mathcal L(H)$ define
\[
 \|T\|_{HS}^2:=\sum_{j=1}^\infty \|T\varphi^{(j)}\|^2.
\]
If this sum is finite, then $T$ is called a \emph{Hilbert--Schmidt operator}.

cards.front: State the theorem of Hilbert--Schmidt space.
cards.back:
The Hilbert--Schmidt operators form a Hilbert space with inner product
\[
 \langle T,S\rangle_{HS}:=\sum_{j=1}^\infty \langle T\varphi^{(j)},S\varphi^{(j)}\rangle,
\]
and the Hilbert--Schmidt norm is independent of the chosen orthonormal basis.

cards.front: State the theorem of Hilbert--Schmidt embedding.
cards.back:
Every Hilbert--Schmidt operator is compact; indeed,
\[
 \|T\|_{\mathcal L(H)}\le \|T\|_{HS}.
\]

cards.front: State the theorem of Spectral theorem for compact symmetric operators.
State the theorem of Low-rank approximation / spectral expansion.
cards.back:
For a compact symmetric operator with eigenpairs $(\varphi^{(j)},\lambda_j)$,
\[
 A = \sum_{j=1}^\infty \lambda_j\, \varphi^{(j)}\otimes \varphi^{(j)},
\qquad
 \|A\|=|\lambda_1|,
\]
and the truncated sums give optimal finite-rank approximations in the operator norm / Hilbert--Schmidt norm formulations recorded in the notes.

cards.front: State the definition of Singular values.
cards.back:
For a compact operator $K$, the \emph{singular values} are
\[
 \sigma_j(K)=\sqrt{\lambda_j(K^*K)},
\]
where $\lambda_j(K^*K)$ are the eigenvalues of the positive compact operator $K^*K$.

cards.front: State the theorem of SVD for compact operators.
cards.back:
Let $K\in\mathcal L(H)$ be compact. Then there exist orthonormal sets $(u_j)$ and $(v_j)$ and singular values $\sigma_j\downarrow0$ such that
\[
 K = \sum_{j=1}^\infty \sigma_j\, u_j\otimes v_j,
\qquad
 K^* = \sum_{j=1}^\infty \sigma_j\, v_j\otimes u_j.
\]
If additionally $K$ is self-adjoint then the left and right singular vectors coincide up to signs.

cards.front: State the definition of Weak derivatives and Sobolev norm in one dimension.
cards.back:
For $u:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$, weak derivatives are defined distributionally. The $H^1$-norm is
\[
 \|u\|_{H^1}^2 = \int_{\mathbb R}|u(x)|^2\,dx + \int_{\mathbb R}|u'(x)|^2\,dx.
\]
Likewise,
\[
 H^1(\mathbb R)=\{u\in L^2(\mathbb R): u'\in L^2(\mathbb R)\}.
\]

cards.front: State the theorem of Sobolev--Fourier characterization of $H^1$.
cards.back:
A function $u\in L^2(\mathbb R)$ belongs to $H^1(\mathbb R)$ iff
\[
 \int_{\mathbb R}(1+|\omega|^2)|\widehat u(\omega)|^2\,d\omega<\infty,
\]
and this quantity is equivalent to the $H^1$ norm.

cards.front: State the theorem of One-dimensional interpolation inequality.
cards.back:
For suitable exponents (as written in the notes),
\[
 \|u\|_{L^q} \le \|u\|_{L^2}^{\theta}\,\|u\|_{H^1}^{1-\theta}
\]
with the exponent relation determined by $q$ and $\theta$.

cards.front: State the theorem of Gagliardo--Nirenberg inequality, 1D form.
cards.back:
For $1\le p<q<\infty$ and appropriate $\alpha\in(0,1)$,
\[
 \|u\|_{L^q} \le C\,\|u\|_{H^1}^{\alpha}\|u\|_{L^p}^{1-\alpha}.
\]

cards.front: State the definition of Jordan normal form.
cards.back:
For $A\in\mathbb C^{n\times n}$, a \emph{Jordan normal form} is a block diagonal matrix
\[
 J=\operatorname{diag}(J_{n_1}(\lambda_1),\dots,J_{n_k}(\lambda_k))
\]
similar to $A$, where each Jordan block is
\[
 J_m(\lambda)=
 \begin{pmatrix}
 \lambda & 1 & & 0\\
 & \lambda & \ddots & \\
 & & \ddots & 1\\
 0 & & & \lambda
 \end{pmatrix}.
\]

cards.front: State the definition of Characteristic polynomial, algebraic and geometric multiplicity.
cards.back:
For $A\in\mathbb C^{n\times n}$, the \emph{characteristic polynomial} is
\[
 \chi_A(z)=\det(zI-A).
\]
If $\lambda$ is an eigenvalue, its \emph{algebraic multiplicity} is its multiplicity as a root of $\chi_A$, and its \emph{geometric multiplicity} is
\[
 \dim\ker(A-\lambda I).
\]

cards.front: State the definition of Spectral radius.
cards.back:
For $A\in\mathbb C^{n\times n}$, the \emph{spectral radius} is
\[
 \rho(A):=\max\{ |\lambda| : \lambda\in \sigma(A)\}.
\]

cards.front: State the theorem of Basic spectral-radius bounds.
cards.back:
For any induced matrix norm,
\[
 \rho(A)^k \le \rho(A^k) \le \|A^k\| \le \|A\|^k.
\]
In particular, $\rho(A)\le \|A\|$.

cards.front: State the theorem of Adapted norm near the spectral radius.
cards.back:
For every $\delta>0$, there exists an induced matrix norm such that
\[
 \rho(A)\le \|A\|\le \rho(A)+\delta.
\]

cards.front: State the theorem of Gelfand formula.
cards.back:
For any $A\in\mathbb C^{n\times n}$ and any induced matrix norm,
\[
 \rho(A)=\lim_{k\to\infty}\|A^k\|^{1/k}.
\]

cards.front: State the definition of Operator power series.
cards.back:
Let $(X,\|\cdot\|)$ be a Banach space and $A\in\mathcal L(X)$. If
\[
 f(z)=\sum_{j=0}^\infty a_j z^j
\]
has radius of convergence $r$, define
\[
 f(A)=\sum_{j=0}^\infty a_j A^j,
\]
whenever $\|A\|<r$.

cards.front: State the theorem of Convergence of operator power series.
cards.back: If $\|A\|<r$, then the series defining $f(A)$ converges in $\mathcal L(X)$.

cards.front: State the theorem of Matrix functional calculus from Jordan form.
cards.back:
For $A\in\mathbb C^{n\times n}$ with Jordan decomposition $A=SJS^{-1}$ and for sufficiently smooth/analytic $f$, one has
\[
 f(A)=Sf(J)S^{-1},
\]
where on each Jordan block $J_m(\lambda)$,
\[
 f(J_m(\lambda))=
 \begin{pmatrix}
 f(\lambda) & f'(\lambda) & \frac{f''(\lambda)}{2!} & \cdots \\
 & f(\lambda) & f'(\lambda) & \ddots \\
 & & \ddots & \ddots \\
 & & & f(\lambda)
 \end{pmatrix}.
\]

cards.front: State the theorem of Riesz representation theorem.
cards.back:
Let $H$ be a Hilbert space. For every $f\in H^*$ there exists a unique $w_f\in H$ such that
\[
 f(v)=\langle w_f,v\rangle \qquad \forall v\in H,
\]
and
\[
 \|w_f\|=\|f\|_{H^*}.
\]
Hence $H^*\cong H$ isometrically.

cards.front: State the definition of Bilinear form.
cards.back: A map $B:H\times H\to\mathbb R$ is a \emph{bilinear form} if it is linear in each variable.

cards.front: State the theorem of Inner product generated by a bilinear form.
cards.back:
If $B$ is bounded, coercive, and symmetric on a Hilbert space $H$, then
\[
 \langle u,v\rangle_B := B(u,v)
\]
defines an inner product on $H$, and the induced norm is equivalent to the original norm on $H$.

cards.front: State the theorem of Lax--Milgram.
cards.back:
Let $H$ be a Hilbert space and let $B:H\times H\to\mathbb R$ be bilinear, bounded, and coercive. Then for every $f\in H^*$ there exists a unique $u\in H$ such that
\[
 B(u,v)=f(v) \qquad \forall v\in H,
\]
and
\[
 \|u\|_H \le \alpha^{-1}\|f\|_{H^*},
\]
where $\alpha>0$ is the coercivity constant.

cards.front: State the definition of Boundary value problem in weak form.
cards.back:
In the one-dimensional model problem on $D=(a,b)$ with $a(x)\ge a_{\min}>0$, the weak bilinear form is
\[
 B(u,v)=\int_D a(x)u_x(x)v_x(x)\,dx,
\]
and the boundary value problem is recast as: find $u\in H_0^1(D)$ such that
\[
 B(u,v)=f(v) \qquad \forall v\in H_0^1(D).
\]
